questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A. A group of organisms that look similar but cannot interbreed
B. A group of organisms that can interbreed and produce offspring
C. Any collection of organisms living in the same habitat
D. A group of unrelated organisms that share similar behaviors

questions.explanation: A species is defined as a group of organisms that can interbreed and produce fertile offspring.

questions.text: 2. Prions are infectious diseases known to cause:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A. Athlete’s foot
Herpes simplex
Mad cow disease
Mumps and measles

questions.explanation: Prions are misfolded proteins that can cause normal proteins to misfold, leading to a chain reaction that damages nerve cells and causes fatal neurodegenerative diseases in humans and animals. Examples are mad cow disease, Kuru, and scrapie.

questions.text: 3. During the lytic cycle, the function of lysozymes released by phase is to
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Synthesise viral genome
Dissolve bacterial cell wall
C. Break down bacterial DNA
D. Speed up the formation of proteins

questions.explanation: Lysozyme breaks down the cell wall's peptidoglycan layer during the lysis phase, leading to the host cell bursting and releasing progeny virions to infect other cells.

questions.text: 4. Synthesis and maturation of phage along with release of new phages constitutes a cycle called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A. Lysis cycle
B. Life cycle
C. Lytic cycle
D. Elliptical cycle

questions.explanation: The lytic cycle is the destructive path of a bacteriophage. The virus takes over the host cell's machinery to rapidly make new virus particles. This process ends when the host cell bursts (lyses), releasing the new viruses.

questions.text: 5. At which stage of HIV infection does one usually show symptoms of AIDS?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A. Within 15 days of sexual contact with an infected person
B. When the infected retrovirus enters host cells
C. When HIV damages large number of helper T-lymphocytes
D. When the viral DNA is produced by reverse transcriptase

questions.explanation: When the viral load has destroyed enough of these cells, the body can no longer fight off opportunistic infections and certain cancers, leading to the clinical symptoms of AIDS.

questions.text: 6. RNA viruses appear to have higher rates of mutation because
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A. RNA nucleotides are more unstable than DNA nucleotides
B. Replication of their nucleic acid does not involve the proofreading steps of DNA replication
C. RNA viruses replicate faster
D. RNA viruses respond more to mutagens

questions.explanation: RNA replication is performed by RNA-dependent RNA polymerases (RdRp), which generally lack this proofreading mechanism. The lack of proofreading means errors are incorporated into the viral RNA genome frequently, leading to a much higher mutation rate.

questions.text: 7. Which of the following statements are true about the capsomeres
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A. It is an individual unit of the capsid
B. It is a viral protein for replication
C. It is a unit of nucleic acid in viruses
D. All of the above

questions.explanation: A capsomere is a protein subunit that forms the viral capsid, the protective protein shell of a virus. They self-assemble to form the capsid.

questions.text: 8. Which of the following is the main function of the capsid?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A. Determines the antigenic specificity of the virus
B. Protects genetic material from nuclease attack
C. Both A and B
D. None of the above

questions.explanation: The viral capsid (protein shell) has two essential roles: it protects the inner genetic material from being destroyed by host cell nucleases, and its surface proteins serve as antigens, which determine the virus's specific recognition by the host's immune system.

questions.text: 9. The first vaccine against smallpox contained
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A. Live cowpox virus
B. Dead cowpox virus
C. Live smallpox virus
D. Weakened smallpox virus

questions.explanation: The live virus was initially sourced from cows (cowpox) and was used to inoculate people, providing immunity to small pox

questions.text: 10. Identify the disease from the given clues: viral infection inflammation of the liver fatigue jaundice transmitted by contact with faeces
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A. Hepatitis A
B. Hepatitis B
C. Kaposi’s sarcoma
D. Septicaemia

questions.explanation: The disease shown in the flowchart, which is a viral infection causing inflammation of the liver (hepatitis) and is transmitted via the fecal-oral route (contact with feces/contaminated food/water), is Hepatitis A. Hepatitis A is a viral infection causing inflammation of the liver and is transmitted via the fecal-oral route. Common symptoms include jaundice, fever, fatigue, loss of appetite, nausea, and abdominal discomfort.

questions.text: 11. Which of the following statements is incorrect?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The infective constituent in viruses is the protein coat.
B. Viruses are obligate parasites
C. Prions consist of abnormally folded proteins
D. Viroids lack a protein coat

questions.explanation: The infective constituent in viruses is nucleic acid, whether DNA or RNA, not the protein coat.

questions.text: . If bacteria and viruses are cultured together in vitro in agar jelly on two separate petri dishes, which one would grow faster?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A. Bacteria will grow and virus will show no growth
B. Bacteria will grow faster than viruses
C. Viruses will grow faster than bacteria
D. Both will grow with same speed

questions.explanation: bacteria are living prokaryotes that only need nutrition and suitable conditions to survive. However, viruses are acellular particles that are obligate parasites, i.e., they need a host. Viruses replicate by controlling the biosynthetic machinery of their host, and they cannot replicate on their own, even when suitable conditions and nutrition are provided in vitro (outside a living organism, in an artificial medium

questions.text: Smith is a pre-medical student. He took his father, aged 55, to the hospital. His father has been experiencing neurological and behavioral issues for quite a while. The doctors examined and diagnosed him with a disease called Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease. Smith recalls from his book that prion particles cause this disease. These are the other facts he remembers about prions: 1.Prions are protein-only particles2.They are smaller in size than both viroids and viruses; 3.Prions can also cause mad cow disease in humans. Which of the given statements are correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A. I only
B. I and II only
C. I and III only
D. All of these

questions.explanation: Prions are misfolded protein particles, which can cause neurodegenerative diseases in humans and animals. It is also smaller than viroids and viruses

questions.text: . What is the sequence of steps in which a bacteriophage attacks bacteria and injects its own DNA?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Landing → Tail contraction → Penetration → DNA Injection
B. Penetration → Landing → Tail contraction → DNA Injection
C. Tail contraction → Landing → DNA Injection → Penetration
D. Landing → Penetration → Tail contraction → DNA Injection

questions.explanation: firstly, the virus lands on a bacterial cell. It then attaches to a receptor site on the bacterial host's cell wall. Once attached completely, the tail contracts and the virus binds to the bacteria irreversibly. During penetration, the tail releases lysozymes to digest the cell wall, and the tail core is forced inside the bacterial cell. The viral DNA is injected into the cell

questions.text: 15. Which of the following is true about viral survival?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A. Non-lipid enveloped viruses are more vulnerable than lipidenveloped viruses
B. RNA viruses have more endurance than DNA viruses
C. A pH of around 6.5 destroys virus’s structure
D. Non-enveloped virus can withstand high humidity

questions.explanation: Non-enveloped virus (adenoviruses, rhinoviruses) can withstand and tolerate high humidity. They are also less vulnerable to environmental changes than enveloped viruses. pH of around 6.5 is favorable for viral survival. RNA viruses have less endurance due to the unstable nature of RNA. Lipid-enveloped viruses have a fragile outer membrane (the envelope) derived from the host cell. This envelope is easily disrupted by desiccation (drying), detergents and heat
